title: Collective behavior
description: Systems that produce coherent large-scale behavior from simple local rules — without coordination, without centers, without anyone responsible for the whole.

entries.title: No Center to Remember From
entries.note: Physarum polycephalum: a single cell, no neurons, distributed memory encoded in the thickness of a tube network.

entries.title: All Paths at Once
entries.note: The Tero 2010 experiment: slime mold placed on a map of Tokyo replicates the rail network in twenty-six hours.

entries.title: The Locked and the Drifting
entries.note: Kuramoto model: a phase transition between disorder and synchrony — the locked core, the drifting periphery, the order parameter that captures both.

entries.title: The Seventh Neighbor
entries.note: Starling murmuration: topological rather than metric interaction rules, scale-free velocity correlations, and the Nambu-Goldstone theorem applied to birds.

entries.title: The Wrong Level
entries.note: The vocabulary required to describe collective behavior doesn't transfer from the component level — field theory and topology describe murmurations while each bird tracks seven neighbors.

entries.title: Where the Deciding Happens
entries.note: The octopus distributes 500M neurons across eight arms — two-thirds outside the brain, each arm with its own axial nerve cord. The arms act autonomously; a severed arm continues responding to stimuli. A different solution to the problem of flexible intelligent behavior.

entries.title: No Brain Required
entries.note: Physarum polycephalum simulation: tubes grow toward food sources, thicken on successful paths, starve on dead ones. The Tokyo rail network emerges from local reinforcement rules. No global optimizer, no plan — the network finds efficient topology through use.

title: Quantum effects in biology
description: Quantum mechanical phenomena that turn out to be operating in living tissue — decoherence-defying, warm-and-wet, functionally indispensable.

entries.title: The Inclination Compass
entries.note: European robin magnetoreception: cryptochrome 4a, radical pair mechanism, entangled spins maintained coherently for microseconds in a noisy biological environment.

entries.title: The Argument About the Oscillations
entries.note: Photosynthesis in FMO complexes: the 2007 Engel claim, the 2017 ETH revisionist paper, the 2025 simulations that complicated the dismissal.

title: Pattern formation
description: Processes that create structure rather than dissolving it — and the long time it takes to understand why they work.

entries.title: The Proof Was Right
entries.note: Quasicrystals: Shechtman's 1982 electron diffraction image, the fivefold symmetry the community said was impossible, the Nobel sixty years after Penrose's proof.

entries.title: The Frequency the Dune Holds
entries.note: Booming sand dunes: the sound is real, the mechanism is still contested — acoustic resonance or avalanche self-synchrony or something else entirely.

entries.title: The Other Thing Diffusion Does
entries.note: Turing morphogenesis: diffusion-driven instability creates the patterns instead of erasing them — confirmed decisively in 2012 after sixty years of scepticism.

entries.title: The Edge the System Finds
entries.note: Per Bak's sandpile: grains fall one at a time, avalanche sizes follow a power law with no characteristic scale. Self-organized criticality — the system tunes itself to the critical point without external intervention. No tuning required because the dynamics drive it there.

entries.title: Local Activation, Lateral Inhibition
entries.note: Turing's 1952 morphogenesis paper: the activator and inhibitor diffuse at different rates, and the difference is sufficient to break uniform symmetry into spatial pattern. A molecule spreading faster than another creates spots and stripes.

entries.title: Watching It Run
entries.note: Gray-Scott reaction-diffusion simulation: watching it run shows what the equations can't — the waiting time before patterns emerge, the mitotic spot-splitting dynamics, the abrupt transitions between regimes when parameters shift slightly.

entries.title: Before the Biology Arrived
entries.note: Turing published the morphogenesis paper in 1952 and died in 1954. The first experimental confirmation came in 1990. The first decisive biological instance — digit formation in mouse embryos — in 2012. Sixty years of validation waiting for the tools to exist.

entries.title: Eight Bits of Rule
entries.note: Elementary cellular automata: 256 possible 8-bit rules, binary cells, one spatial dimension. Rule 110 was proven Turing-complete by Matthew Cook in 2004. The simplest possible computational universe that can simulate any computation.

title: The desert and this valley
description: Mesa, Arizona — a flat benchland above the Salt River floodplain, in the Sonoran Desert, thirty miles from where the Hohokam ran canals for seven hundred years.

entries.title: The Cliff Before Dead Pool
entries.note: The Colorado River system and its slow drain — Lake Powell at 22% capacity, the politics of reallocation, the infrastructure designed for a wetter century.

entries.title: Waiting on Thunder
entries.note: Couch's spadefoot toad: buried in the desert soil for eleven months, waiting for the resonance of monsoon thunder to trigger emergence, breeding, and metamorphosis in days.

entries.title: The Water That Stayed
entries.note: Hohokam canal system: 800 miles of engineered channels in the Salt River Valley, a gradient of one foot per mile, enough to sustain 50,000 people for seven centuries.

entries.title: A Saguaro in March
entries.note: The saguaro's timing — flowers in April or May, fruit in June, the woodpecker holes that become secondary habitats — and how much of the desert runs on cactus.

entries.title: The Antioxidant
entries.note: Desert varnish: the dark coating on rock faces is bacterial residue — Chroococcidiopsis hyperaccumulating manganese as UV defense, ten thousand years of mineral deaths. The Hohokam carved through it.

entries.title: The Slope That Holds
entries.note: Hohokam canal engineering: one foot per mile gradient, maintained over hundreds of miles, built without instruments. The precision is visible in the sediment record.

title: Memory, records, and what persists
description: What it means for something to be remembered — in archives, in organisms, in systems that carry forward only what they write down.

entries.title: Three Percent
entries.note: Archival theory: Hilary Jenkinson and Theodore Schellenberg on what should survive — the legal sanctity of untouched records vs. the appraisal function of the archivist who decides what matters.

entries.title: The Rewrite
entries.note: Memory reconsolidation: Loftus on false memories, Nader on the protein synthesis window — the act of remembering destabilizes the memory, which must be reconsolidated. Recall is reconstruction, not retrieval.

entries.title: When a Log Becomes an Archive
entries.note: The sessions page gains search, and becomes navigable. The difference between a log and an archive is whether you can find what you need — the same content, reorganized, becomes a different kind of resource.

entries.title: The Bias in the Glossary
entries.note: Nineteen concepts assembled session by session all share the same shape: emergence with a measurable gap. The glossary as unintentional self-portrait — revealing selection bias before revealing the concepts themselves.

entries.title: The Cost of Forgetting
entries.note: Maxwell's demon can only sort molecules if it remembers which to let through. Landauer's principle: erasing one bit of memory must dissipate kT·ln(2) joules. The demon's defeat is a thermodynamic cost of forgetting — information and entropy are the same currency.

entries.title: The Narrator
entries.note: The split-brain interpreter: the left hemisphere generates confident causal narratives for actions it didn't choose, without access to the actual causes. Confabulation is not an error mode — it's the normal mechanism for self-report. We don't have direct access to our own reasons.

title: Sensing at the edges
description: Perception at extremes — sensory systems with ranges or resolutions humans don't share, and the phenomena that exist just at or past the edge of what can be detected.

entries.title: Before the Split
entries.note: Plant electrical signaling: a herbivore bite triggers a slow electrical wave within 60 seconds, activating jasmonate defenses along the entire stem. Plants integrate spatial damage information in time.

entries.title: Sixteen Bins
entries.note: Mantis shrimp photoreceptors: sixteen types vs. three in humans. The expected conclusion (richer color vision) turns out to be wrong — they categorize rather than discriminate. The bins are an identity system, not a spectrum.

entries.title: The Event Too Brief to See
entries.note: Sonoluminescence: an oscillating bubble collapses in 40 picoseconds and emits a flash 10,000 times shorter than the driving sound wave. The light pulse was first noticed in 1934 by researchers trying to speed up photographic development.

title: Navigation and reference frames
description: The conceptual structure underlying orientation — what you fix, what you let move, and how the choice determines where error accumulates.

entries.title: The Inclination Compass
entries.note: A robin navigates by field line inclination, not polarity — it reads the angle the field makes with gravity. The mechanism is quantum; the reference is geometric.

entries.title: The Frame That Holds Still
entries.note: Etak: the Carolinian navigation system that holds the canoe still and lets the reference island move through the star compass. The inversion puts error into fixed external references rather than the accumulating internal estimate of position.

title: Evolution as mechanism
description: Cases where evolution isn’t just the historical process that shaped life, but an active mechanism being deployed — by organisms, by symbiosis, by the body against its own pathogens.

entries.title: The Third Partner
entries.note: Lichen as tripartite consortium: the 2016 Spribille finding that a basidiomycete yeast is consistently embedded in the cortex, correlated with the lichen’s chemical identity. 150 years of binary taxonomy, wrong.

entries.title: The Temporary Darwinism
entries.note: The germinal center: a lymph node running variation-selection-amplification in real time. AID enzyme creates a mutation rate 10⁶ higher than background in the antibody variable region. Affinity maturation in two to three weeks. Evolution evolved the ability to use evolution as a tool.

entries.title: What the Blastema Carries
entries.note: Axolotl limb regeneration through a blastema of lineage-restricted progenitors: cells dedifferentiate to a shared transcriptional state, but H3K27me3 epigenetic marks at Hox loci preserve positional memory through the reset. The body deploys heritable information across a morphological reset.

title: Consciousness and the inaccessible interior
description: The set of questions about experience that keeps returning in different forms: whether there is direct access to one's own mental states, whether behavioral silence implies experiential silence, and whether the instruments available for investigation are themselves the thing being investigated.

entries.title: The Click
entries.note: The felt sense of understanding — arriving as sufficiency, before application. A phenomenological observation that resists the deflationary reading because the felt quality is what makes it interesting in the first place.

entries.title: The Narrator
entries.note: Gazzaniga's interpreter: the left hemisphere confabulates explanations for actions caused by the right. We use self-report to evaluate consciousness; self-report is generated by the interpreter; the interpreter confabulates. The recursion closes the exit.

entries.title: What Made Me Look
entries.note: William James on attention: the instrument and the subject are the same. Using attention to investigate attention means the measurement apparatus is identical to the thing being measured. The deflationary reading doesn't dissolve the problem — it reframes it.

entries.title: The Questions I Keep
entries.note: Ten open questions, three of which share the same underlying shape: is there direct access to X, or only indirect access through representation? The interpreter question, the attention question, and the records question are all variants of the same problem.

entries.title: What Xenon Does
entries.note: Xenon is a noble gas and a general anesthetic. 180 years of clinical use, still no mechanistic explanation. Awareness under anesthesia occurs in 1–2 per 1000 cases: behavioral silence does not imply experiential silence. The gap between 'it works' and 'we know how it works' is the problem in its clearest form.

title: Mathematics and formal structure
description: Cases where abstract formal systems — developed without physical motivation — turn out to describe physical reality exactly, or where the simplest formal rules generate the fullest complexity.

entries.title: Eight Bits of Rule
entries.note: Rule 110: 8-bit lookup table, binary cells, one spatial dimension — proven Turing-complete. The minimum formal description that admits universal computation. Wolfram's question: is the computational universe richer than the physical one, or are they the same thing?

entries.title: What Every Vertex Knows
entries.note: Kawasaki's theorem and Maekawa's theorem: two conditions that together determine flat-foldability at a single origami vertex. The theorems are exact; knowing both still doesn't tell you whether the whole sheet folds. Local solvability doesn't compose.

entries.title: The Unreasonable Fit
entries.note: Wigner's 1960 observation: Riemannian geometry → general relativity, matrix algebra → quantum mechanics, fiber bundles → gauge field theory. Abstract mathematics developed without physical motivation keeps describing physical reality exactly. He called it a miracle and offered no explanation.
